pattern_recognition: Dynamic Programming, as the problem involves finding a subsequence that satisfies certain conditions. Signals include the need to compare elements and build solutions based on previously computed results.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Generate all possible subsequences and check which ones are strictly increasing, then return the length of the longest one.
approaches.algorithm: Use recursion to explore all subsequences, keeping track of the longest increasing subsequence found.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Utilize dynamic programming combined with binary search to efficiently find the length of the longest increasing subsequence.
approaches.algorithm: Maintain an array to track the smallest tail of all increasing subsequences of various lengths, using binary search to update this array.

dry_run: Consider the input nums = [10, 9, 2, 5, 3, 7, 101, 18]. Start with an empty array for the longest subsequence. As we iterate through nums, we find that 2 can extend the sequence, then 3 can extend it further, followed by 7, and finally 101. The longest increasing subsequence is [2, 3, 7, 101], which has a length of 4.
edge_cases: An empty input should return 0. A single element input should return 1. Duplicates should be handled correctly, as they do not contribute to the increasing sequence. Large inputs may require efficient algorithms to avoid timeouts.
common_pitfalls: 1) Confusing subsequences with substrings; subsequences do not require elements to be contiguous. 2) Failing to account for duplicates; they should not be included in the increasing sequence. 3) Incorrectly implementing the binary search, leading to wrong updates in the tail array.
